title: Distributed Software Architecture Postgraduate Review Map

sections.heading: Four questions, not fifteen subjects
sections.content: Instead of memorising every subject, organise the programme around four questions: how do users and systems connect? (front end, APIs, mobile); where do state and data live? (databases, messaging, data platforms); how do we deliver and operate? (cloud, DevOps, observability); and how do we protect and evolve? (Zero Trust, governance, AI, ADRs). Architecture is choosing boundaries and trade-offs under constraints. The programme gives vocabulary; learning becomes durable when that vocabulary helps you decide something concrete.

sections.heading: Interfaces, back end, and multiple platforms
sections.content:
Front end covers experience, rendering, state, accessibility, performance, and browser security. Back end covers modularity, contracts, business rules, data, and integrations. Mobile, PWA, and cross-platform systems add unreliable connectivity, portability, and release management.

Exercise: model an order journey across three screens and define its HTTP contract, validation, authentication, cache, and offline behaviour. Ask what belongs to the client, API, or domain.

sections.heading: Cloud, data, and operations
sections.content: Cloud-native is not synonymous with Kubernetes: it means designing for automation, external configuration, telemetry, and failure. Serverless trades operational work for execution limits, latency constraints, and cost. Data architecture requires choices about consistency, model, governance, and ingestion before tools. DevSecOps makes build, tests, infrastructure, and security repeatable. Start observability with the OpenTelemetry primer: traces show a request path, metrics show trends, and logs provide context.

sections.heading: Distribution and security
sections.content: Microservices help only when business boundaries and independent operations justify their complexity. Events bring decoupling but require idempotency, versioning, retry, DLQ handling, and eventual consistency. IoT and edge add devices and intermittent networks. Zero Trust replaces implicit network trust with identity, policy, and least privilege for every resource. Read NIST SP 800-207 and the event-driven architecture guide.

sections.heading: AI, governance, and architecture projects
sections.content:
AI solutions need data, evaluation, security, cost control, monitoring, and governance; a chatbot is not a complete AI architecture. Generative AI can accelerate analysis and alternatives, but never removes human validation or accountability.

An architecture project joins requirements, quality attributes, diagrams, ADRs, risks, metrics, and evolution. Social impact, privacy, accessibility, and professional responsibility are requirements, not an appendix.

sections.heading: Six-week recovery plan
sections.content:
Week 1: model a modular monolith and quality attributes. Week 2: expose an API and design data consistency. Week 3: add an event and idempotency. Week 4: package, deploy through CI/CD, and instrument. Week 5: threat-model and apply least privilege. Week 6: write ADRs and present trade-offs.

At the end of every week, write one page: decision, rejected alternative, risk, and measurement. This turns attended course material into recoverable knowledge.

sections.heading: A POC answers one question
sections.content: Do not build a miniature application. Define a hypothesis, experiment, metric, and decision. • Hypothesis: events with an outbox provide consistency between services. • Experiment: persist an order, publish an event, simulate duplicates and an outage. • Metrics: duplicates processed, p95 delay, and recovery after restart. • Decision: adopt, reject, or reduce uncertainty with another POC. The deliverable is a README, run script, results, and ADR — not disposable code without a conclusion.

sections.heading: Practical decision tree
sections.content: Which risk is most expensive? • Cross-service consistency → outbox, idempotency, and replay POC. • Latency or scale → load test with p95/p99, errors, and saturation. • External dependency → adapter, timeout, retry, and simulated failure. • Sensitive access → identity, least privilege, and denial test. Do not prove that a technology works. Discover whether it satisfies the system’s real constraints.

sections.heading: Trade-off matrix
sections.content:
ChoiceGainCostMinimum POC
Synchronous RESTsimple flowtemporal couplingtimeout and load
Eventsdecouplingeventual consistencyoutbox and duplicate
Modular monolithsimple operationsrequires boundary disciplineboundary tests
Microservicesselective autonomynetwork and operationsobservable end-to-end flow

sections.heading: Learning kit
sections.content: Use one tool at a time: ADRs to record a decision; OpenTelemetry for traces, metrics, and logs; a load test for capacity; threat modelling for abuse and controls; and a chaos drill for dependency failures. Architectural skill means turning uncertainty into evidence.
